article_title: History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery
label: Fig. 1
caption: Artist’s illustration showing the exposure of the anterolateral brainstem, vertebral artery, basilar region, and the lower cranial nerves afforded by the far-lateral approach. © Department of Neurosurgery, University of Utah

article_title: History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery
label: Fig. 5
caption: Variation in bony exposure of the far-lateral approach (FLA). This figure demonstrates adaptations in bony exposure to different targets along the craniovertebral junction. A, B Intraoperative images showing a more conservative variation of the FLA, omitting C1 laminectomy and minimizing condylar drilling to preserve occipitocervical stability, while still allowing exposure of the lower cranial nerves, vertebral artery (open arrow), and proximal posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA, asterisk) segment for microsurgical clipping of vertebral and PICA aneurysms. V3, third segment of vertebral artery; SS, sigmoid sinus; CF, condylar fossa; FM, foramen magnum. Panels A and B are reproduced without changes from Sriamornrattanakul et al. [47] under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0, https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/). C, D Artist’s illustrations depicting the ELTO (extreme lateral transcondylar transodontoid) approach, which is performed to access lesions extending beyond the midline. The ELTO approach involves transposition of the vertebral artery followed by removal of the posterior and lateral aspects of the occipital condyle, drilling of the lateral mass of C1, and resection of the anterior arch of C1 to fully expose the odontoid and ventral craniovertebral junction. This trajectory (arrow) provides a direct corridor to midline and contralateral lesions. Panels C and D © Department of Neurosurgery, University of Utah

article_title: History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery
label: Fig. 6
caption: Examples of cases in which extensions of the far-lateral approach (FLA) are utilized. A This adolescent patient had a massive clival chordoma with brainstem compression. The ELTO approach was utilized to enable removal of the tumor bilaterally through a left-sided approach (two upper panels). The two lower panels demonstrate the postoperative images. B This 36-year-old male patient presented with gait ataxia and quadriparesis. A FLA was used with extension down into the cervical spine to enable decompression of the brainstem and upper cervical spine to the C5 level (three upper panels). The three lower panels demonstrate the postoperative appearance and the bone removal used during tumor removal

article_title: History and evolution of the far-lateral approach in neurosurgery
label: Fig. 7
caption: Variation in closure technique following the far-lateral approach. A Postoperative photograph showing a curvilinear far lateral skin closure using running nylon sutures. B Axial CT image showing the fat and fascia graft placed within the resection cavity, visible as a well-defined hypodense area. Panels A and B reproduced with permission from Scoville et al. [44]. (C, D) Intraoperative photographs depicting an alternative closure method utilizing chevron drill holes. C The trajectory of suture needles passed through the chevron holes drilled into the occipital bone. D The fascia and muscular layers are anchored upward through the chevron holes, pulling the myocutaneous flap tightly against the bone to enhance dural protection and multilayer closure integrity. Panels C and D reproduced with permission from Ali et al. [3]
